facts.text: Equestrian is the only Olympic sport in which men and women compete directly against each other on equal terms.
Secretariat set the still-unbroken Kentucky Derby record of 1:59.4 in 1973, winning by 2½ lengths.
A horse's hoof is essentially a single toe — horses are perissodactyls, or odd-toed ungulates.
The Thoroughbred breed traces back to three foundation stallions: the Byerly Turk, the Darley Arabian, and the Godolphin Arabian.
Western saddles weigh 25–60 pounds, while English saddles typically weigh only 10–25 pounds.
Polo is sometimes called 'the sport of kings' and has been played for over 2,000 years.
Horses can sleep both lying down and standing up, thanks to a 'stay apparatus' in their legs that locks their joints.
The Kentucky Derby has been held annually at Churchill Downs in Louisville, Kentucky since 1875.
Charlotte Dujardin became the most decorated female Olympic equestrian athlete with six medals across three Games.
The Lipizzaner stallions of the Spanish Riding School in Vienna have been trained in classical dressage since 1572.
A farrier typically replaces horseshoes every 6 to 8 weeks as the hoof grows.
Barrel racing is a rodeo event predominantly competed in by women and requires incredible speed and agility from both horse and rider.
Horses have the largest eyes of any land mammal and can see nearly 360 degrees around them.
The Preakness Stakes, the second leg of the Triple Crown, is held at Pimlico Race Course in Baltimore.
Seabiscuit, an undersized Thoroughbred, became a symbol of hope during the Great Depression and was the top U.S. newsmaker in 1938.
Dressage, from the French word for 'training,' is often described as horse ballet.
The Arabian horse is one of the oldest breeds in the world, with archaeological evidence dating back over 4,500 years.
The Calgary Stampede, billed as 'The Greatest Outdoor Show on Earth,' has been held since 1912.
A horse's heart weighs about 9–10 pounds and can pump roughly 75 liters of blood per minute during exercise.
Show jumping courses can have obstacles up to 1.70 meters (5 feet 7 inches) high at the Grand Prix level.
The Clydesdale breed originated in Scotland and became famous through the Budweiser Clydesdale advertising campaigns.
Horses were first domesticated on the steppes of Central Asia around 4000 BCE.
The Belmont Stakes, the final leg of the Triple Crown, is held at Belmont Park in Elmont, New York.
Isabell Werth of Germany is the most successful Olympic dressage rider ever, with multiple gold and silver medals.
A horse can run within hours of being born, an evolutionary trait for escaping predators.
Eventing, also called 'horse triathlon,' combines dressage, cross-country, and show jumping in a single competition.
The Quarter Horse got its name from being the fastest breed over a quarter-mile distance.
Prince Charles was a dedicated polo player for decades before retiring from the sport due to injuries.
English riding uses a smaller, lighter saddle that allows closer contact between rider and horse.
Horses have approximately 205 bones in their skeleton.
The Pony Express operated from April 1860 to October 1861, covering nearly 2,000 miles between Missouri and California.
Royal Ascot is a prestigious five-day race meeting attended by the British Royal Family each June.
Black Beauty, published by Anna Sewell in 1877, was one of the first novels written from an animal's perspective.
Frankel, a British racehorse, retired unbeaten after 14 races and is considered one of the greatest racehorses ever.
A horse's teeth take up more space in its head than its brain does.
Vaulting is the discipline of performing gymnastics and dance on horseback while the horse moves on a lunge line.
The Friesian horse from the Netherlands is known for its striking black coat and feathered fetlocks.
Endurance riding competitions can cover 50 to 100 miles in a single day over varied terrain.
American Pharoah became the 12th Triple Crown winner in 2015, ending a 37-year drought.
The Melbourne Cup is Australia's most famous horse race, known as 'the race that stops a nation.'
Horses communicate through a complex system of vocalizations, body language, and facial expressions.
McLain Ward is one of the most successful American show jumpers, with two Olympic gold medals in team events.
A horse typically drinks 5 to 10 gallons of water per day, and more in hot weather or during exercise.
The Appaloosa is known for its distinctive leopard-spotted coat pattern and was bred by the Nez Perce people.
Professional Bull Riders (PBR) events feature riders attempting to stay on a bucking bull for eight seconds.
The Andalusian horse, also known as the Pure Spanish Horse, has been prized for its athleticism since the 15th century.
Beezie Madden is the only show jumper to win back-to-back World Cup Finals and holds multiple Olympic medals.
Horses can gallop at speeds up to 55 mph, with the fastest recorded speed being 43.97 mph over a sustained distance.
The snaffle bit is the most common type of bit and works by applying direct pressure to the corners of the horse's mouth.
Cavalry units were a dominant military force for thousands of years until mechanized warfare made them obsolete in the 20th century.
The first equestrian events at the modern Olympics were held in 1900 in Paris.
Zara Tindall, granddaughter of Queen Elizabeth II, won a silver medal in eventing at the 2012 London Olympics.
A horse's field of vision covers about 350 degrees, with two small blind spots directly in front of and behind them.
Reining is a Western riding discipline where the horse performs spins, slides, and stops guided by subtle rider cues.
Mustangs are free-roaming horses of the American West, descended from horses brought by Spanish explorers.
Grooming a horse before riding helps stimulate blood flow, removes dirt, and allows the rider to check for injuries.
The novel 'War Horse' by Michael Morpurgo was adapted into both a stage play and a Steven Spielberg film.
Argentina has produced some of the world's best polo players, including Adolfo Cambiaso, the highest-rated player ever.
Horses are herbivores with a digestive system designed for small, frequent meals of forage like hay and grass.
The Grand National at Aintree is one of the most famous steeplechase races in the world.
A horse's hoof grows about 6–9 mm per month and requires regular trimming even without shoes.
Show jumping was the first equestrian discipline to be included in the Olympics, debuting in 1912.
The Cheyenne Frontier Days rodeo has been held annually in Wyoming since 1897.
Horses have excellent long-term memory and can remember people and places for years.
The sport of polo requires a minimum of four players per team riding on a grass field up to 300 yards long.
Man o' War won 20 of 21 career races and was voted the greatest racehorse of the 20th century by the Associated Press.
An adult horse's brain weighs about 22 ounces, roughly half the weight of a human brain.
Cross-country in eventing tests a horse's stamina and courage over solid obstacles on natural terrain.
Riding helmets must meet safety standards such as ASTM/SEI certification to protect against head injuries.
The Budenny horse was developed in Russia as a military mount and is now used for show jumping and eventing.
Spirit: Stallion of the Cimarron was a 2002 animated film about a wild Mustang in the Old West.
Horses have 16 muscles in each ear, allowing them to rotate their ears 180 degrees independently.
The Kentucky Derby is known as 'The Most Exciting Two Minutes in Sports.'
Western riding originated from the cattle-working traditions of Spanish vaqueros in the American West.
The Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe is Europe's most prestigious flat horse race, held at Longchamp, Paris.
A stirrup iron should be one inch wider than the widest part of the rider's boot for safety.
Horses can recognize human emotions from facial expressions and voice tone.
The Hidalgo movie (2004) told the story of a Mustang competing in a 3,000-mile endurance race across the Arabian Desert.
The average lifespan of a domestic horse is 25 to 30 years, though some live well into their 40s.
Affirmed was the last horse to win the Triple Crown before American Pharoah, accomplishing it in 1978.
A dressage arena measures either 20×40 meters (small) or 20×60 meters (large) and is marked with letters around its perimeter.
The Akhal-Teke horse from Turkmenistan has a distinctive metallic sheen to its coat that earned it the nickname 'golden horse.'
Calf roping, also called tie-down roping, requires a rider to lasso a calf, dismount, and tie three of its legs.
Horses produce approximately 50 pounds of manure per day.
The first Breeders' Cup was held in 1984 at Hollywood Park Racetrack.
Equine-assisted therapy uses horses to help people with physical, emotional, and cognitive challenges.
The Morgan horse is one of the earliest horse breeds developed in the United States, descending from a single stallion named Figure.
A bridle consists of the headstall, bit, and reins, and is the primary means of communication with the horse's head.
Nick Skelton won Olympic show jumping gold at age 58 at the 2016 Rio Games, one of the oldest Olympic equestrian champions.
Horses can't vomit due to the strong muscles of their lower esophageal sphincter.
The Dubai World Cup is one of the richest horse races in the world, with a purse of $12 million.
Mounted police units still operate in major cities worldwide, using horses for crowd control and community policing.
The Hanoverian is one of the most successful warmblood breeds in Olympic dressage and show jumping.
A horseshoe is traditionally considered a symbol of good luck when hung above a doorway.
The Spanish Riding School's Lipizzaner stallions are born dark and gradually turn white as they mature.
Hans Günter Winkler of Germany won five Olympic gold medals in show jumping between 1956 and 1972.
Equestrian vaulting was a demonstration sport at the 1920 Olympics in Antwerp.
A horse's fetlock joint is analogous to the ball of a human foot, not the ankle as commonly believed.
The Paint Horse is known for its combination of white and dark coat patterns, and is one of the most popular breeds in the U.S.
Polo ponies are not actually ponies — any horse used for polo is called a 'pony' regardless of height.
The Epsom Derby has been run since 1780 and is one of the five British Classics.
A horse's lungs can hold about 55 liters of air, allowing for tremendous cardiovascular capacity during exercise.
The Percheron draft horse from France was used extensively for hauling and farm work before motorized equipment.
Barrel racing patterns form a cloverleaf shape around three barrels set in a triangular formation.
Mr. Ed, the talking horse from the 1960s TV show, was actually a palomino gelding named Bamboo Harvester.
Horses have two blind spots: one directly in front of their forehead and one directly behind their tail.
Michael Jung of Germany is the only rider to have simultaneously held the Olympic, World, and European eventing titles.
A well-fitted saddle distributes the rider's weight evenly to prevent pressure points and soreness on the horse's back.
The Tennessee Walking Horse is known for its unique four-beat 'running walk' gait.
The first recorded polo match was played in Persia (modern-day Iran) around 600 BC.
Secretariat's heart was found to weigh an estimated 22 pounds at necropsy — about 2.5 times the average horse heart.
Equestrian events at the 1956 Melbourne Olympics were held in Stockholm, Sweden due to Australian quarantine laws.
A hand, the unit used to measure horse height, equals 4 inches.
The Paso Fino horse from Latin America is prized for its smooth, natural four-beat gait.
Side reins, draw reins, and martingales are auxiliary equipment used to help develop a horse's frame during training.
Horses are social animals that form strong bonds within herds and can become stressed when isolated.
The first Grand National steeplechase was run in 1839 at Aintree Racecourse in Liverpool.
A foal can stand and walk within one to two hours of being born.
Dressage was included in the Olympic Games starting in 1912 in Stockholm.
The Trakehner horse originated in East Prussia and is one of the lightest and most refined warmblood breeds.
Racehorses in the Northern Hemisphere all share the same official birthday of January 1.
Riding boots with a heel of at least one inch prevent the foot from sliding through the stirrup.
Winx, an Australian mare, won 33 consecutive races between 2015 and 2019.
The Haflinger breed from Austria is known for its chestnut color and flaxen mane and tail.
Horses can detect vibrations in the ground through their hooves before humans can feel an approaching animal or vehicle.
The United States Equestrian Federation (USEF) is the national governing body for equestrian sports in America.
A horse can eat approximately 1.5 to 2 percent of its body weight in forage per day.
Three-day eventing was originally designed to test military horses for versatility and stamina.
The Marwari horse from India is known for its distinctive inward-curving ear tips.
Sidesaddle riding was developed in the Middle Ages to allow women in dresses to ride more modestly.
Horses have a nearly 65-degree range of binocular vision directly in front of them.
The Badminton Horse Trials is one of the most prestigious eventing competitions in the world.
A girth or cinch secures the saddle to the horse's body by wrapping under the belly.
The Shire horse holds the record for the tallest horse breed, sometimes exceeding 20 hands (80 inches) in height.
Justify won the Triple Crown in 2018, becoming only the second undefeated Triple Crown winner after Seattle Slew.
Horses use a 'flehmen response' — curling their upper lip — to better detect scents.
The Hambletonian Stakes is the most prestigious race in American harness racing.
Catherine the Great of Russia was an accomplished rider who often rode astride rather than sidesaddle.
The Connemara pony from Ireland is the country's only native breed and excels in show jumping.
A horse's cannon bone (lower leg) has no muscles — only tendons, ligaments, and bone.
Natural horsemanship emphasizes understanding horse psychology to build trust rather than using force.
The miniature horse stands under 34–38 inches tall but is classified as a horse, not a pony.
Hunter/jumper competitions judge both the horse's form and technique over fences.
The Standardbred horse is the primary breed used in harness racing and was developed in North America.
George Morris is known as the father of American show jumping and coached the U.S. team for decades.
A curry comb is a rubber or metal tool used in circular motions to loosen dirt and dried mud from a horse's coat.
Horses typically have 40 teeth — 24 molars, 12 incisors, and 4 canines (usually only in males).
The first U.S. Triple Crown was won by Sir Barton in 1919.
Draft horses like the Clydesdale, Percheron, and Belgian were bred for heavy farm and industrial work.
The World Equestrian Games are held every four years and feature eight equestrian disciplines.
A breastplate prevents the saddle from sliding backward, especially useful in eventing and fox hunting.
Wild horses still roam in areas of the American West, Australia (brumbies), and Namibia.
Horseback archery was practiced by steppe nomads like the Mongols and Huns centuries ago.
A horse's cannon bone can bear forces of over 10,000 pounds per square inch at a gallop.
The Rolex Grand Slam of Show Jumping awards a bonus to any rider who wins three consecutive major Grand Prix events.
The Irish Draught is the foundation breed for many successful Irish Sport Horses used in international competition.
Rein-back in dressage requires the horse to walk backward in a straight, diagonal two-beat movement.
The Tevis Cup is a 100-mile endurance ride through California's Sierra Nevada mountains, considered one of the toughest.
Horses can carry roughly 20% of their body weight comfortably — about 200–250 lbs for an average horse.
The Knabstrupper horse from Denmark is known for its spotted coat patterns, similar to the Appaloosa.
Jockeys typically weigh between 108 and 118 pounds and must maintain strict weight limits.
Lunging involves working a horse in a circle on a long line, often used for training and exercise.
The Turkoman horse, now extinct, was one of the ancestors of the modern Thoroughbred.
Rodrigo Pessoa of Brazil is the only show jumper to have won the World Cup Final, Olympic gold, and World Championship.
A stock saddle used in Australian bush riding combines elements of both English and Western saddle design.
Horses can develop colic, a potentially life-threatening condition involving abdominal pain, from various digestive issues.
The first modern dressage competition was held at the 1902 Turin Exhibition in Italy.
The Przewalski's horse is the only truly wild horse species still in existence — all others are feral.
A sport horse's daily diet may include hay, grain, vitamins, minerals, and electrolytes depending on workload.
The Selle Français is a French warmblood developed specifically for show jumping and eventing.
Mounted games (gymkhana) include relay races, flag competitions, and other speed events on horseback.
The British Horse Society was founded in 1947 and promotes horse welfare and riding standards.
A horse can rotate each ear independently to focus on different sounds, helping them monitor their environment.
The Kentucky Oaks, held the day before the Derby, is one of the premier races for three-year-old fillies.
Flying changes in dressage involve the horse switching its leading leg at the canter without breaking gait.
The Cleveland Bay is the oldest established horse breed in England and was originally a pack horse.
A brumby is the Australian term for a feral horse, and culling programs remain controversial.
Equine chiropractors and massage therapists are increasingly used in competitive horse care.
The first Kentucky Derby was won by a horse named Aristides in 1875.
A horse's digestive system is about 100 feet long and food can take 65–75 hours to pass through completely.
The passage in dressage is a highly collected, elevated trot with marked suspension between each diagonal pair.
Arabian horses have one fewer vertebra, one fewer rib, and one fewer tail bone than other breeds.
Charreada is a traditional Mexican rodeo that includes nine competitive events showcasing horsemanship skills.
The Burghley Horse Trials in England are one of the six CCI5* five-star eventing competitions in the world.
Half-pass is a lateral dressage movement where the horse moves forward and sideways simultaneously.
Horses can learn to respond to a vocabulary of verbal commands and are capable of basic problem-solving.
The Dartmoor pony is a hardy native breed from England that has lived on the moors for centuries.
Steeplechase racing involves jumping fixed fences over distances of two to four miles.
Ian Millar of Canada, known as 'Captain Canada,' competed in a record 10 Olympic Games in show jumping.
A saddle pad or numnah is placed under the saddle to absorb sweat and prevent rubbing.
The Gypsy Vanner horse, also called the Gypsy Cob, was bred by Romani travelers in Ireland and Britain.
Piaffe in dressage is a highly collected trot performed essentially in place, demonstrating maximum impulsion.
Trail riding is the most popular equestrian activity in the United States.
A horse's temperature should normally be between 99 and 101.5 degrees Fahrenheit.
The Prix Saint-Georges is the first international-level dressage test a horse and rider compete in.
The Welsh Cob and Welsh Pony breeds are divided into four sections (A through D) based on height.
Horseball is a team sport combining elements of rugby, basketball, and polo played on horseback.
The Mongol Derby, at 1,000 kilometers, is the world's longest horse race and follows the route of Genghis Khan's postal system.
A horse's eye can switch between monocular and binocular vision depending on the position of its head.
Seattle Slew was the only undefeated Triple Crown winner until Justify in 2018.
Equine dentistry is a specialized field — a horse's teeth continuously erupt and require regular floating (filing).
The Exmoor pony is one of the oldest and most primitive horse breeds in the world.
Combined driving is a three-phase competition consisting of dressage, marathon, and cone driving.
Racehorses can reach speeds of 40 mph and their stride can cover over 20 feet at a full gallop.
The half-halt is a subtle riding aid that rebalances the horse by briefly increasing pressure from seat, legs, and hands.
Olympic dressage tests include movements judged on a scale of 0 to 10 by multiple judges around the arena.
The Dutch Warmblood (KWPN) has dominated international show jumping and dressage rankings.
Cutting is a Western competition where a horse separates a single cow from a herd, requiring minimal rider input.
Count Robert Toptani of Albania brought equestrian sports to the early Olympic movement.
The Cheltenham Festival is a major National Hunt horse racing event held annually in March.
A horse can hear sounds at frequencies up to 25,000 Hz, much higher than the human range of 20,000 Hz.
The puissance is a show jumping competition focused on jumping the highest single wall, sometimes exceeding 7 feet.
Fox hunting on horseback dates back to the 16th century in England, though drag hunting now often replaces live quarry.
The Oldenburg horse from Germany is commonly used in international dressage competitions.
A standing martingale prevents a horse from raising its head above a desired level.
The 2012 London Olympics were the first to feature dressage set to freestyle music with individual choreography.
The Icelandic horse is known for its two extra gaits, the tölt and the flying pace.
Horse racing in ancient Rome featured chariot races in the Circus Maximus, which could hold 250,000 spectators.
The hackamore is a bitless bridle that applies pressure to the nose and chin rather than the mouth.
The Lusitano horse from Portugal excels in classical dressage and bullfighting demonstrations.
Team penning requires three riders to separate three designated cattle from a herd in the fastest time.
Horses have been used in therapy for people with disabilities since the 1960s through hippotherapy programs.
The Breeders' Cup Classic is the pinnacle race of the Breeders' Cup World Championships.
A floating ring bit rotates in the horse's mouth, encouraging the horse to relax its jaw and accept the contact.
Skijoring involves a horse pulling a skier over snow and is popular in Scandinavia and parts of the American West.
The Holsteiner is one of the oldest warmblood breeds, originating from the Schleswig-Holstein region of Germany.
Horses' hooves act as shock absorbers and blood pumps, helping circulate blood back up the legs.
The ASPCA was founded in 1866 largely in response to the mistreatment of carriage and work horses.
Valegro, ridden by Charlotte Dujardin, set the dressage world record score of 94.3% in freestyle at the 2014 Olympics.
A horse can produce 10 gallons of saliva per day to aid in the digestion of dry forage.
The Federation Equestre Internationale (FEI) governs all international equestrian sports and was founded in 1921.
The Nokota horse is a rare breed descended from horses of the Northern Great Plains.
Tent pegging is a cavalry sport in which a rider at a gallop uses a lance to pick up a small ground target.
The USET Foundation supports the training and competition of U.S. equestrian athletes.
Riders at the Spanish Riding School train for at least 8 years before performing publicly with the Lipizzaner stallions.
A horse can't breathe through its mouth; it's an obligate nasal breather.
Royal Ascot's dress code requires top hats and formal attire in the Royal Enclosure.
The Suffolk Punch is the oldest native draft breed of Britain, always chestnut in color.
Cowboy dressage blends classical dressage principles with Western horsemanship traditions.
The Kentucky Horse Park in Lexington is a working horse farm, educational theme park, and competition facility.
Jingang, ridden by Alex Hua Tian, helped China qualify for Olympic eventing for the first time.
Horses can run at about 4 months old and reach 80% of their adult size by 2 years.
The Grand Pardubice Steeplechase in the Czech Republic is one of the most dangerous races in the world.
A curb chain on a Western bit applies pressure under the horse's chin to encourage flexion at the poll.
The Rocky Mountain Horse from Kentucky is known for its chocolate coat and flaxen mane.
Competitive trail riding (CTR) judges the condition of the horse throughout a timed distance ride.
Mark Todd of New Zealand was twice named 'Horseman of the 20th Century' by the FEI.
Clipping a horse's winter coat helps prevent overheating during hard work in cold weather.
A healthy horse's resting heart rate is approximately 28–44 beats per minute.
Working equitation is a discipline that tests classical dressage skills applied to practical cattle-working tasks.
The Saddlebred, also known as the American Saddlebred, is famed for its high-stepping gait and show presence.
The 2024 Paris Olympics featured equestrian events at the Palace of Versailles grounds.
A running martingale has rings through which the reins pass, applying downward pressure when the horse raises its head too high.
The flying lead change is a movement where a cantering horse switches its leading legs without breaking stride.
Horses form deep pair bonds, and separating bonded horses can cause significant stress and behavioral issues.
The Belgian horse is the most popular draft breed in the United States.
Flat racing, where horses run on level ground without jumps, is the most common form of horse racing worldwide.
The Fjord horse from Norway is one of the world's oldest breeds, recognizable by its unique dun color and stripe.
Jousting, a medieval equestrian combat sport, is the official state sport of Maryland.
The Rolex Kentucky Three-Day Event is the only CCI5* eventing competition in the Western Hemisphere.
Horses have a 'stay apparatus' in both their front and hind legs that allows them to lock their joints and rest standing up.
A double bridle uses two bits — a bradoon and a curb — and four reins, used in advanced dressage.
John Henry, a gelding, won the Arlington Million twice and earned over $6.5 million in his career.
Trakehner brand marks feature the distinctive double moose antler symbol on the left hip.
Mounted archery competitions are growing worldwide, with events in South Korea, Hungary, and the United States.
A horse's withers (the ridge between the shoulder blades) is where height is measured.
Alexander the Great's horse Bucephalus was one of the most famous horses in history, carrying him through his conquests.
Horses can drink up to 15 gallons of water per day in hot weather or during intense exercise.
The National Horse Show has been held in the United States since 1883 and is one of the oldest indoor horse competitions.
The Franches-Montagnes is the last remaining native Swiss horse breed.
Equine influenza is a highly contagious respiratory disease that can spread rapidly through stables.
Philippe Le Jeune of Belgium won individual show jumping gold at the 2010 World Equestrian Games.
The posting trot involves the rider rising and sitting in rhythm with the horse's diagonal gait.
Horses have no collarbone, which allows for a greater range of motion in their front legs.
Pieter Devos and McLain Ward have been perennial fixtures atop the show jumping world rankings.
The Camargue horse from southern France is known for being born dark and turning white as it matures.
The piaffe and passage are considered the most difficult movements in Grand Prix dressage.
Lead rope training starts when horses are foals and builds the foundation for all future handling.
The Preakness infield party is one of the largest outdoor gatherings in American sports.
A horse's frog — the triangular structure on the bottom of the hoof — helps absorb shock and pump blood.
The sport of polocrosse combines elements of polo and lacrosse and originated in Australia.
The Westphalian horse from Germany has produced numerous Olympic and World Championship medalists in dressage.
A lunging cavesson is a specialized halter with a reinforced noseband used during groundwork.
Trec (Techniques de Randonnée Equestre de Compétition) is a trail-based competition originating in France.
Desert Orchid, a beloved British racehorse, won the King George VI Chase four times.
Horses have about 700 muscles, comprising approximately 60% of their total body weight.
The Japan Cup is one of Asia's most prestigious flat races, held at Tokyo Racecourse.
Roll-back turns in Western reining require the horse to stop, pivot 180 degrees, and immediately lope off.
The Don horse from Russia was the mount of choice for Cossack cavalry.
A horse blanket comes in various weights, from lightweight sheets for fly protection to heavy-fill turnout blankets for winter.
Pony Club, founded in 1929 in the United Kingdom, teaches young people horsemanship worldwide.
Seattle Slew won the Triple Crown in 1977 and later became a highly successful breeding stallion.
The average horse takes 12–20 breaths per minute at rest.
Kentucky has more horses per capita than any other U.S. state.
The Hackney horse is a high-stepping carriage breed known for its flashy trot.
International eventing uses a star rating system from CCI1* to CCI5* to indicate difficulty level.
Spanish walk is a dressage movement where the horse extends each front leg high and forward alternately.
The Crabbet Arabian stud in England was instrumental in developing the modern Arabian horse breed.
An average riding lesson lasts 30 to 60 minutes, with beginners usually starting at a walk and trot.
The Palio di Siena is a bareback horse race held twice each summer in Siena, Italy.
Tongue ties are used in racing to prevent a horse from getting its tongue over the bit during a race.
The Missouri Fox Trotter is known for its smooth 'fox trot' gait, ideal for trail riding over rough terrain.
Hippology is the study of horses, covering everything from anatomy and physiology to breed characteristics.
The National Finals Rodeo (NFR) is the premier professional rodeo championship held annually in Las Vegas.
Cross-country jumps in eventing are solid and do not fall down when hit, unlike show jumping rails.
Anky van Grunsven of the Netherlands won three consecutive Olympic dressage gold medals from 2000 to 2008.
The Fell Pony from England's Lake District was used as a pack horse and for farm work.
An oxer is a type of show jumping fence with two rails set at different heights and widths.
The term 'hands' for measuring horses comes from the width of a man's hand, standardized to 4 inches.
Citation was the first racehorse to earn $1 million in career prize money.
The Alltech FEI World Equestrian Games 2010 in Kentucky were the first held outside Europe.
A horse's small intestine is about 70 feet long, while the large intestine is about 25 feet.
The Mangalarga Marchador from Brazil is the most popular breed in South America.
The rollkur technique in dressage, involving hyperflexion of the horse's neck, has been controversial.
Riders use leg yields to teach horses to move sideways away from the pressure of the rider's leg.
The National Velvet (1944) starring Elizabeth Taylor is one of the most famous horse movies ever made.
Horses can distinguish between happy and angry human facial expressions.
The Curragh Racecourse in Ireland has hosted horse racing since the 1700s.
A surcingle is a strap that goes around a horse's girth area, used in lunging and vaulting.
The Canadian Horse is a heritage breed descended from horses sent to New France by King Louis XIV.
Dressage letters around the arena (A, K, E, H, C, M, B, F) have been used since the 18th century, though their origin is debated.
The Royal Windsor Horse Show has been held in the grounds of Windsor Castle since 1943.
A horse's dock is the fleshy top part of its tail, made up of the coccygeal vertebrae.
Point-to-point racing is amateur steeplechasing open primarily to horses that have been regularly hunted.
The Welsh Section D, or Welsh Cob, is popular for both riding and driving competitions.
Longeing with side reins helps develop a horse's balance and encourages the horse to work over its back.
Ben Maher of Great Britain won individual show jumping gold at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics.
Horses groom each other by standing side by side and nibbling each other's withers, called mutual grooming.
The oldest recorded horse, Old Billy, lived to be 62 years old in 19th-century England.
The Argentine Open Polo Championship in Buenos Aires is considered the most prestigious polo tournament.
A flash noseband wraps around the horse's chin to prevent it from opening its mouth too wide.
The New Forest Pony has roamed England's New Forest for over a thousand years.
Collection in riding means engaging the horse's hindquarters to carry more weight, lightening the forehand.
Laura Kraut became the first woman to win the show jumping World Cup Final in 2005.
Horses are hindgut fermenters, meaning they digest fibrous food in the cecum and large colon.
Lexington, Kentucky, known as the 'Horse Capital of the World,' hosts over 450 horse farms.
Bitless bridles, including the bosal and sidepull, are preferred by some riders who want no bit contact.
The Bashkir Curly horse has a uniquely curly coat and is sometimes tolerated by people with horse hair allergies.
Pony Express riders were typically lightweight young men, and their rides averaged 75 to 100 miles each.
The Hickstead Derby in England features a famous 10-foot-6-inch bank that horses must jump down.
The age of a horse can be estimated by examining the wear patterns and shape of its teeth.
Aachen CHIO is considered the unofficial world championship of show jumping, held annually in Germany.
Leg wraps and boots protect a horse's lower legs from injury during work and transport.
The Orlov Trotter is a Russian breed developed by Count Alexei Orlov in the late 18th century.
A water complex in cross-country eventing tests a horse's bravery as it jumps into, through, or out of water.
Gene Autry's horse Champion and Roy Rogers' horse Trigger were among the most famous Hollywood horses.
The Budweiser Clydesdales have been an advertising icon since 1933, when they celebrated the repeal of Prohibition.
A horse's third eyelid (nictitating membrane) helps protect and clean the eye.
Pentathlon originally included equestrian show jumping as one of its five disciplines until 2024.
The Brumby Heritage Act of 2018 in Australia aimed to protect wild horses in certain national parks.
Shoulder-in is a lateral dressage movement where the horse moves with its shoulders to the inside of the track.
The San Siro racecourse in Milan hosts the Gran Premio di Milano, one of Italy's premier races.
A horse can focus on different objects with each eye independently using monocular vision.
Equine recurrent uveitis (moon blindness) is the most common cause of blindness in horses.
The Welsh Mountain Pony (Section A) is one of the most popular children's riding ponies in the world.
The canter pirouette in dressage requires the horse to maintain the canter rhythm while turning nearly in place.
A no-climb fence is the safest type of fencing for horse pastures, preventing hooves from getting caught.
Totilas, a Dutch Warmblood stallion, broke dressage world records in 2009 and became a global sensation.
Kentucky Derby winners are draped with a garland of 554 red roses, hence the race's nickname 'Run for the Roses.'
Horses can sense fear and anxiety in humans through changes in body language and potentially through scent.
The Ride for the Brand is a traditional saying in ranch culture meaning loyalty to the outfit you work for.
The Danish Warmblood has risen to international prominence in both dressage and show jumping.
A bounce or no-stride combination in show jumping requires the horse to jump two fences with no canter stride between them.
Edwin 'Ted' Edgar was a legendary British show jumping course designer renowned for challenging courses.
The Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo is the world's largest livestock exhibition and rodeo.
Horses have a specialized structure called the digital cushion in their hooves that helps absorb concussion.
The Godolphin Arabian, one of the three foundation sires of the Thoroughbred, was reportedly rescued from pulling a water cart in Paris.
Paddock boots and half chaps are an affordable alternative to tall riding boots for everyday riding.
The Pegasus World Cup at Gulfstream Park has offered a purse of up to $16 million.
Horses can sense electromagnetic fields and use the earth's magnetic field to help navigate.
The pirouette in reining is called a spin and requires the horse to rotate 360 degrees or more around its inside hind leg.
Laminitis, an inflammation of the laminae in the hoof, is one of the most serious conditions in horses.
The Spruce Meadows Masters in Calgary is one of the richest show jumping tournaments in the world.
George Stubbs, the 18th-century English painter, was famous for his anatomically precise paintings of horses.
A horse's average body temperature is 100°F (37.8°C), slightly higher than a human's.
The phrase 'get back on the horse' originated from the genuine advice given to fallen riders.
Red Rum won the Grand National three times (1973, 1974, 1977) and is considered the greatest steeplechaser.
The Nokota horse was nearly driven to extinction before being discovered in Theodore Roosevelt National Park.
Horses sweat primarily through their skin, unlike dogs which pant, making electrolyte management critical in hot weather.
The World Polo Championship is held every three to four years, with Argentina dominating the competition.
A horse's stomach is relatively small for its body size, holding only about 2–4 gallons, which is why they need to eat frequently.
Nijinsky was the last horse to win the English Triple Crown (2000 Guineas, Derby, St Leger) in 1970.
The Travers Stakes at Saratoga, known as the 'Midsummer Derby,' is the oldest major stakes race in the U.S.
Horses can recognize themselves in mirrors, suggesting a degree of self-awareness.
